persons.arguments_against: There are 3 McCaigs in the staff lists, so a relative could in principle hold one of these posts, and the earliest records (1913-15) are printed with a single initial 'D. McCaig'. This fails because the bio describes one unusual two-phase career and BOTH stints reconstruct it. The bio: 'clk., Fiji, 1912; Post-mstr., 1929; regraded postal clk., 1938; supt. prisons, 1947'. The 1913 stint shows the postal half exactly — Postal Department clerk from 1913 rising to Postmaster by 1930. The 1950 stint shows the prisons half — Superintendent of Prisons 1950-56, matching 'supt. prisons, 1947', with 6 editions co-occurring. The combination of a postal career THEN a prison superintendency is a unique signature; a relative would not replicate both halves under the same D. F. initials. The single-initial early records simply expand to D. F. by 1917 within the same continuous Postal Department run.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second D. F. McCaig in the Fiji postal service or prison service, splitting the two phases between two men.

notes: Two stints jointly reconstruct the bio's unusual postal-then-prisons career; rare surname (3 in lists), two-initial match, and the unique Superintendent of Prisons post (6 editions) make both certain.
